subject: Mathematics
q: If $2x - y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -3 & 0 \\ 3 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $2y + x = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$, then which of the following is correct?
question: If $2x - y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -3 & 0 \\ 3 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $2y + x = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$, then which of the following is correct?
solution:
We have two matrix equations:

$$2x - y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -3 & 0 \\ 3 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad x + 2y = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$4x - 2y = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -6 & 0 \\ 6 & 6 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now add this to the second equation $x + 2y$. The $-2y$ and $+2y$ cancel:

$$5x = \begin{bmatrix} 6+4 & -6+1 & 0+5 \\ 6-1 & 6+4 & 4-4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & -5 & 5 \\ 5 & 10 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This already matches Option B.

Using $x + 2y = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$,

$$2y = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 & -4 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 4 \\ -2 & 2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

So

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Notice this is almost like Option D, but the entry in the first row third column is $2$, not $-2$. So Option D is slightly off.

For $x + y$:

$$x + y = \begin{bmatrix} 2+1 & -1+1 & 1+2 \\ 1-1 & 2+1 & 0-2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Option A shows $-1$ in the first row third place instead of $3$, so it does not match.

For $x - y$:

$$x - y = \begin{bmatrix} 2-1 & -1-1 & 1-2 \\ 1+1 & 2-1 & 0+2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Option C shows $3$ in place of $-1$, so it does not match either.

So the only correct choice here is Option B, since

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$ and $kA = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3a \\ 2b & 24 \end{bmatrix}$, then the values of $k, a, b$ are respectively
question: If $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$ and $kA = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3a \\ 2b & 24 \end{bmatrix}$, then the values of $k, a, b$ are respectively
solution:
$$kA=\begin{bmatrix}0 & 3a \\ 2b & 24\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Rightarrow k\begin{bmatrix}0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0 & 3a \\ 2b & 24\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix}0 & 2k \\ 3k & -4k\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0 & 3a \\ 2b & 24\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Rightarrow 2k=3a,\quad 3k=2b,\quad -4k=24$$

$$\Rightarrow a=\frac{2k}{3},\quad b=\frac{3k}{2},\quad k=-6$$

$$\therefore a=-4,\quad b=-9,\quad k=-6$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A - 2B = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 5 \\ 3 & 7\end{bmatrix}$ and $2A - 3B = \begin{bmatrix}-2 & 5 \\ 0 & 7\end{bmatrix}$, then matrix $B$ is equal to
question: If $A - 2B = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 5 \\ 3 & 7\end{bmatrix}$ and $2A - 3B = \begin{bmatrix}-2 & 5 \\ 0 & 7\end{bmatrix}$, then matrix $B$ is equal to
solution:
We have

$$ A-2B=\begin{bmatrix}1&5\\3&7\end{bmatrix} $$

and

$$ 2A-3B=\begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} $$

From the first equation,

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix}1&5\\3&7\end{bmatrix}+2B $$

Now put this value of $A$ into the second equation:

$$ 2A-3B=\begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} $$

so,

$$ 2\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&5\\3&7\end{bmatrix}+2B\right)-3B = \begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} $$

$$ 2\begin{bmatrix}1&5\\3&7\end{bmatrix}+4B-3B = \begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} $$

$$ \begin{bmatrix}2&10\\6&14\end{bmatrix}+B = \begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} $$

$$ B= \begin{bmatrix}-2&5\\0&7\end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix}2&10\\6&14\end{bmatrix} $$

$$ B= \begin{bmatrix}-4&-5\\-6&-7\end{bmatrix} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: $$\begin{bmatrix} x^2 + x & x \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -x+1 & x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ then x is equal to -
question: $$\begin{bmatrix} x^2 + x & x \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -x+1 & x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ then x is equal to -
solution:
For two matrices to be equal, their corresponding elements must be equal.

$$ \begin{bmatrix} x^2 + x & x \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -x+1 & x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x^2+x+0 & x-1 \\ 3+(-x+1) & 2+x \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ \begin{bmatrix} x^2+x & x-1 \\ 4-x & 2+x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

From the top right entry,

$$ x-1=-2 $$

$$ x=-1 $$

Check with other entries:

Top left:

$$ x^2+x = (-1)^2+(-1)=1-1=0 $$

Correct.

Bottom left:

$$ 4-x = 4-(-1)=5 $$

Correct.

Bottom right:

$$ 2+x = 2+(-1)=1 $$

Correct.

So the value of $x$ is

$$ \boxed{-1} $$

Option A.

subject: Mathematics
q: Number of matrices that can be obtained by using one or more numbers from four given numbers is :
question: Number of matrices that can be obtained by using one or more numbers from four given numbers is :
solution:
• Using exactly 1 number:

    We can choose 1 out of the 4 given numbers in ${}^4C_1 = 4$ ways.

    A matrix with exactly 1 element can only have one possible order, which is $1 \times 1$.

    The single chosen number can be arranged in its place in $1! = 1$ way.

    Number of matrices = $4 \times 1 \times 1 = 4$.

  • Using exactly 2 numbers:

    We select 2 numbers out of the 4 in ${}^4C_2 = 6$ ways.

    A matrix with 2 elements can have the orders $1 \times 2$ or $2 \times 1$. This gives us 2 possible orders.

    The 2 chosen numbers can be arranged in their positions in $2! = 2$ ways.

    Number of matrices = $6 \times 2 \times 2 = 24$.

  • Using exactly 3 numbers:

    We select 3 numbers out of the 4 in ${}^4C_3 = 4$ ways.

    A matrix with 3 elements can have the orders $1 \times 3$ or $3 \times 1$. This gives us 2 possible orders.

    The 3 chosen numbers can be arranged in $3! = 6$ ways.

    Number of matrices = $4 \times 2 \times 6 = 48$.

  • Using all 4 numbers:

    We select all 4 numbers in ${}^4C_4 = 1$ way.

    A matrix with 4 elements can have the orders $1 \times 4$, $4 \times 1$, or $2 \times 2$ because these are the combinations of rows and columns that multiply to 4. This gives us 3 possible orders.

    The 4 numbers can be arranged within the matrix in $4! = 24$ ways.

    Number of matrices = $1 \times 3 \times 24 = 72$.

To get the total number of matrices that can be formed, we add the results from all these cases together.

$$Total \ matrices = 4 + 24 + 48 + 72 = 148$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of diagonal matrix A of order n for which $A^3 = A$ is
question: The number of diagonal matrix A of order n for which $A^3 = A$ is
solution:
For a diagonal matrix $A$ of order $n$, write

$$ A=\operatorname{diag}(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n) $$

Now,

$$ A^3=\operatorname{diag}(a_1^3,a_2^3,\dots,a_n^3) $$

Given that

$$ A^3=A $$

so each diagonal entry must satisfy

$$ a_i^3=a_i $$

for every $i=1,2,\dots,n$.

$$ a_i^3-a_i=0 $$

$$ a_i(a_i^2-1)=0 $$

$$ a_i(a_i-1)(a_i+1)=0 $$

So each $a_i$ can be one of

$$ 0,\ 1,\ -1 $$

That means each diagonal position has $3$ choices.

Since there are $n$ diagonal entries, total number of such diagonal matrices is

$$ 3^n $$

So the correct option is

$$ \boxed{3^n} $$

Option D.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $ \begin{bmatrix} x - y & 2x + z \\ 2x - y & 3z + w \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 5 \\ 0 & 13 \end{bmatrix} $, then the value of w is
question: If $ \begin{bmatrix} x - y & 2x + z \\ 2x - y & 3z + w \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 5 \\ 0 & 13 \end{bmatrix} $, then the value of w is
solution:
When two matrices are equal, it means each entry in one matrix matches the entry in the same position of the other matrix. So we can match the corresponding entries and form simple equations.

Matching the entries from $\left[\begin{array}{cc}x-y & 2x+z \\ 2x-y & 3z+w\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-1 & 5 \\ 0 & 13\end{array}\right]$, we get:

$$ x - y = -1, \quad 2x + z = 5 $$

$$ 2x - y = 0, \quad 3z + w = 13 $$

Let's first use the two equations that only involve $x$ and $y$:

$$ x - y = -1 \quad \text{and} \quad 2x - y = 0 $$

Subtract the first from the second to remove $y$. This gives $2x - x = 0 - (-1)$, so $x = 1$.

Now put $x = 1$ back into $x - y = -1$. That gives $1 - y = -1$, so $y = 2$.

Next, use $x = 1$ in the equation $2x + z = 5$. This becomes $2(1) + z = 5$, which gives $z = 3$.

Finally, put $z = 3$ into $3z + w = 13$. This becomes $3(3) + w = 13$, so $9 + w = 13$, giving $w = 4$.

So the required values are $x = 1,\ y = 2,\ z = 3,\ w = 4$, and the value of $w$ is $4$.

subject: Mathematics
question: What must be the matrix $X$, if $2X + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 8 \\ 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ ?
solution:
$$ 2X + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 8 \\ 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ 2X = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 8 \\ 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ 2X = \begin{bmatrix} 3-1 & 8-2 \\ 7-3 & 2-4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ X = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$

So the correct answer is Option A:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$

subject: Mathematics
question: If $2X - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$, then $X$ is equal to
solution:
We have

$$ 2X - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ 2X = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ 2X = \begin{bmatrix} 3+1 & 2+2 \\ 0+7 & -2+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ X = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ \frac{7}{2} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ \frac{7}{2} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

Option C.

subject: Mathematics
q: $\begin{aligned} & \text { A is a square matrix of order } n \times n \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \ell= & \text { max. no. of distinct elements if } A \text { is triangular } \\ m= & \text { max. no. of distinct elements if } A \text { is a diagonal } \\ & \text { matrix } \\ p= & \text { minimum no. of zeroes if } A \text { is triangular matrix. }\end{aligned} \\ & \text { If } \ell+5=p+2 m \text { then find } n \text {. }\end{aligned}$
question: $\begin{aligned} & \text { A is a square matrix of order } n \times n \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \ell= & \text { max. no. of distinct elements if } A \text { is triangular } \\ m= & \text { max. no. of distinct elements if } A \text { is a diagonal } \\ & \text { matrix } \\ p= & \text { minimum no. of zeroes if } A \text { is triangular matrix. }\end{aligned} \\ & \text { If } \ell+5=p+2 m \text { then find } n \text {. }\end{aligned}$
solution:
max. no. of distinct elements if $A$ is triangular

$$\ell = \text{diagonal elements} + \text{below/above diagonal elements} + \text{zero} = n + \frac{n^2-n}{2} + 1 = \frac{n^2+n}{2} + 1$$

max. no. of distinct elements if $A$ is a diagonal matrix

$$m = \text{number of diagonal elements} + \text{zero} = n + 1$$

minimum no. of zeroes if $A$ is triangular matrix =

$$p = \text{no. of non-diagonal elements above/below diagonal} = \frac{n^2-n}{2}$$

given condition $\ell + 5 = p + 2m$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{n^2+n}{2} + 1 + 5 = \frac{n^2-n}{2} + 2(n+1)$$

$$\Rightarrow n^2 + n + 12 = n^2 + 3n + 4$$

$$\Rightarrow n = 4$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If number of elements is 20 then how many different order of matrices can be formed if number of rows is always even?
question: If number of elements is 20 then how many different order of matrices can be formed if number of rows is always even?
solution:
For a matrix with $20$ elements, if it has $m$ rows and $n$ columns, then

$$ m \times n = 20 $$

We need the number of possible orders such that the number of rows is always even.

So we look at all factor pairs of $20$ and keep only those where the first number, that is the number of rows, is even.

The factor pairs of $20$ are:

$$ 1 \times 20,\quad 2 \times 10,\quad 4 \times 5,\quad 5 \times 4,\quad 10 \times 2,\quad 20 \times 1 $$

Now check the number of rows:

  • $1 \times 20$ → rows $=1$ odd, not allowed

  • $2 \times 10$ → rows $=2$ even, allowed

  • $4 \times 5$ → rows $=4$ even, allowed

  • $5 \times 4$ → rows $=5$ odd, not allowed

  • $10 \times 2$ → rows $=10$ even, allowed

  • $20 \times 1$ → rows $=20$ even, allowed

So the possible orders are:

$$ 2 \times 10,\quad 4 \times 5,\quad 10 \times 2,\quad 20 \times 1 $$

Hence, the number of different orders is

$$ 4 $$

subject: Mathematics
q: How many matrices can be formed with elements $\{1,2$, $3,4\}$, if each element is used once?
question: How many matrices can be formed with elements $\{1,2$, $3,4\}$, if each element is used once?
solution:
A matrix can be formed only after deciding its order.

Since there are $4$ distinct elements, the possible matrix orders using all of them exactly once are:

$$1 \times 4,\quad 2 \times 2,\quad 4 \times 1$$

Now count each case.

For a $1 \times 4$ matrix, we just arrange the $4$ elements in a row.

Number of such matrices:

$$4! = 24$$

For a $4 \times 1$ matrix, we arrange the $4$ elements in a column.

Number of such matrices:

$$4! = 24$$

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, the $4$ elements can be placed in the $4$ positions in any order.

Number of such matrices:

$$4! = 24$$

So total number of matrices is

$$24 + 24 + 24 = 72$$

Hence, the number of matrices is

$$\boxed{72}$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 2 & 4 & 3\end{array}\right]$ then find sum of elements of second row of $A+A^T$.
question: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 2 & 4 & 3\end{array}\right]$ then find sum of elements of second row of $A+A^T$.
solution:
Given

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3\\ 0 & 5 & 0\\ 2 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix} $$

So,

$$ A^T=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2\\ 2 & 5 & 4\\ 3 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ A+A^T= \begin{bmatrix} 1+1 & 2+0 & 3+2\\ 0+2 & 5+5 & 0+4\\ 2+3 & 4+0 & 3+3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 5\\ 2 & 10 & 4\\ 5 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$

The second row is

$$ [2,\ 10,\ 4] $$

Its sum is

$$ 2+10+4=16 $$

So, the required sum is $16$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the maximum number of distinct entries in a triangular matrix of order $n$ is 16 , then the value of $n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
question: If the maximum number of distinct entries in a triangular matrix of order $n$ is 16 , then the value of $n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
In a triangular matrix of order $n$, all the entries either above or below the main diagonal are zero.

So, the maximum number of distinct entries will come from the non-zero part together with $0$.

The number of entries in the triangular part including the main diagonal is

$$ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} $$

If all these entries are different, and also $0$ is present in the other half, then the maximum number of distinct entries is

$$ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + 1 $$

$$ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + 1 = 16 $$

$$ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = 15 $$

$$ n(n+1) = 30 $$

$$ n = 5 $$

The value of $n$ is $5$.
